title: Contrastive Novelty Learning: Anticipating Outliers with Large Language Models

structured.summary: The paper presents Contrastive Novelty Learning (CNL), a method to improve open-set selective classification by using large language models to generate out-of-distribution examples and training classifiers to reduce confidence on them while retaining in-distribution accuracy. Achieving significant improvements in AUROC and AUAC, the method is tested on four NLP datasets.

strengths.evidence: The method improves OOD detection by 5.5% AUROC and 2.3% AUAC over prior methods.
The improvements in OOD detection are achieved without detriment to ID accuracy, as stated in the results section.

weaknesses.evidence: The methodology relies on existing elements such as using LLMs for generating examples and contrastive loss, which are not entirely new concepts as evidenced in Section 3.1.

weaknesses.point: Lack of Comprehensive Evaluation on Datasets
weaknesses.evidence: The evaluation is only across 4 NLP datasets, which may not sufficiently represent diverse real-world scenarios as mentioned in Section 3.

weaknesses.point: Insufficient Justification for Choice of Metrics
weaknesses.evidence: AUROC and AUAC are used without discussing why these are the most appropriate measures, as noted in Section 2.2.

weaknesses.point: Clarity Issues in Explanation of CNL
weaknesses.evidence: Sections 3 and 3.1 are convoluted and lack detailed explanations or pseudocode for core elements like contrastive confidence loss.
Reliance on proprietary LLMs like GPT-3 limits reproducibility, and Section 1 mentions forthcoming code release with no specifics available at the time of review.
Section 3.1 assumes that LLMs can accurately represent novel classes without mechanisms to ensure quality or correctness.
High computational demands are noted without extensive discussion on trade-offs in computational cost or time as stated in Sections 3 and beyond.

weaknesses.point: Lack of In-depth Analysis on Negative Results
weaknesses.evidence: Section 'Results' provides average improvements without addressing when and why the method might fail.

weaknesses.point: Potential Data Leakage Not Fully Addressed
weaknesses.evidence: Section 3.1 mentions filtering out known classes but lacks rigorous checks against LLM datasets to ensure compliance.

weaknesses.point: Ethical and Practical Concerns of LLM Use
weaknesses.evidence: The paper does not consider the computational resources and ethical implications of using LLMs, which are critical in real-world application.

weaknesses.point: Lack of Comparison with Full OOD Industrial Tasks
weaknesses.evidence: The paper lacks discussions on translating improvements to complete industrial pipelines, evidenced by a focus on academic benchmarks.

weaknesses.point: Absence of Generalization Strategy Across Domains
weaknesses.evidence: No methods are proposed to generalize across different domains outside of NLP, as there is a lack of cross-domain analyses.

questions.question: How does CNL perform on tasks beyond text classification?
questions.motivation: Understanding the generalization of the method across different types of NLP tasks is critical for assessing its broader applicability.

questions.question: What are the computational costs associated with utilizing large LLMs for generating examples?
questions.motivation: Knowing the trade-offs in terms of computational costs and resource utilization is necessary for practical deployment and scalability.

critique_points.point_001: Novelty of the Approach. The methodology relies on existing elements such as using LLMs for generating examples and contrastive loss, which are not entirely new concepts as evidenced in Section 3.1
critique_points.point_002: Lack of Comprehensive Evaluation on Datasets. The evaluation is only across 4 NLP datasets, which may not sufficiently represent diverse real-world scenarios as mentioned in Section 3
critique_points.point_003: Insufficient Justification for Choice of Metrics. AUROC and AUAC are used without discussing why these are the most appropriate measures, as noted in Section 2.2
critique_points.point_004: Clarity Issues in Explanation of CNL. Sections 3 and 3.1 are convoluted and lack detailed explanations or pseudocode for core elements like contrastive confidence loss
critique_points.point_005: Reproducibility Concerns. Reliance on proprietary LLMs like GPT-3 limits reproducibility, and Section 1 mentions forthcoming code release with no specifics available at the time of review
critique_points.point_006: Assumptions in Example Generation. Section 3.1 assumes that LLMs can accurately represent novel classes without mechanisms to ensure quality or correctness
critique_points.point_007: Scalability and Efficiency Considerations. High computational demands are noted without extensive discussion on trade-offs in computational cost or time as stated in Sections 3 and beyond
critique_points.point_008: Lack of In-depth Analysis on Negative Results. Section 'Results' provides average improvements without addressing when and why the method might fail
critique_points.point_009: Potential Data Leakage Not Fully Addressed. Section 3.1 mentions filtering out known classes but lacks rigorous checks against LLM datasets to ensure compliance
critique_points.point_010: Ethical and Practical Concerns of LLM Use. The paper does not consider the computational resources and ethical implications of using LLMs, which are critical in real-world application
critique_points.point_011: Lack of Comparison with Full OOD Industrial Tasks. The paper lacks discussions on translating improvements to complete industrial pipelines, evidenced by a focus on academic benchmarks
critique_points.point_012: Absence of Generalization Strategy Across Domains. No methods are proposed to generalize across different domains outside of NLP, as there is a lack of cross-domain analyses
